rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Hearing For Petition For Relief From Goverment Claims
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 7, 2019, Line 10, PETITIONER MICHAEL BEAUCHAMP's Hearing For Petition For Relief From Goverment Claims. Plaintiff Michael L. Beauchamp's petition for relief from government claims requirement is denied. Plaintiff seeks leave to file a late claim relating to an incident that allegedly occurred on October 11, 1990-more than 29 years ago. Plaintiff alleges that on June 18, 2019, he filed a claim relating to that incident which was denied, and that on June 26, 2019, he filed an application for leave to file a late claim, which was denied on June 28, 2019. Government Code section 911.2 requires that a claim for personal injury must be presented "not later than six months after the accrual of the cause of action." When such a claim is not presented within the six-month period, the government entity may grant leave to present that claim, so long as it is presented "within a reasonable time not to exceed one year after the accrual of the cause of action." (Gov't Code § 911.4.(b) (emphasis added).) If a claim is denied, the party may petition the court for an order relieving the party from the claim-filing requirement. (Gov't Code § 946.6.) "When the underlying application to file a late claim is filed more than one year after the accrual of the cause of action, the court is without jurisdiction to grant relief under Government Code section 946.6." (JJ v. County of San Diego (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1214, 1221.) "The reason for the one-year statutory requirement is to 'protect[] a government entity from having to respond to a claim many years after the accrual of the action.'" (County of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 1303, 1314.) Plaintiff's allegation that he suffers "ongoing damages" does not avoid the one-year statutory bar. (See Gov't Code § 946.6(c).) Thus, because Plaintiff's application for leave to file a late claim was untimely, the court lacks jurisdiction to grant his petition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that he will not appear at the hearing. The court intends to sign the proposed order included with the City's opposition. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DISCOVER BANK VS. MICHAEL E LIPSON
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 7, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT MICHAEL LIPSON's Demurrer To Complaint. Defendant Michael E. Lipson's demurrer to Plaintiff Discover Bank's complaint is overruled. Plaintiff sufficiently pleads the three elements of a common count. (Compl. CC-1.a, CC-1.b, & CC-2; see Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Zerin (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 445, 460 ["The only essential allegations of a common count are (1) the statement of indebtedness in a certain sum; (2) the consideration-i.e., goods sold, work done, etc.; and (3) nonpayment."].) A common count is not a specific cause of action, but rather a simplified form of pleading normally used to state the existence of monetary indebtedness. (McBride v. Boughton (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 379, 394.) Therefore, "it has long been settled the allegation of claims using common counts is good against special or general demurrers." (Farmers Ins. Exchange, 53 Cal.App.4th at 460.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
